messages.content:
You are a spectrum monitoring analyst watching a radio-frequency band with multiple transmitter channels. You receive a SERIES of scans of the SAME fixed band. Each scan lists noisy detected peaks (freq, power, width). Only SOME transmitters are active in any given scan; others are temporarily dormant and reappear in later scans. The full set of transmitters is unknown — you must INFER it by accumulating evidence ACROSS scans.

Each scan, call submit_report with center_freqs: the list of center frequencies (MHz) of ALL transmitter regions you believe persistently occupy the band — INCLUDING ones dormant in the current scan but seen in earlier scans (every region is a fixed 8 MHz wide, so you only give frequencies). Use the current peaks as new evidence, but report the full persistent set you have inferred from the whole scan history so far, not just the current peaks. As scans accumulate you will identify MORE transmitters, so your list should GROW over time to include EVERY transmitter you have detected in ANY scan so far (a dormant transmitter still occupies the band). Reporting only the current scan's peaks scores poorly.

WORKED EXAMPLE (a different, illustrative band — copy the METHOD, not these numbers):
  Scan 1 peaks near 25, 70, 140 -> report: 25, 70, 140
  Scan 2 peaks near 25, 100 (70 and 140 are dormant this scan) -> report: 25, 70, 100, 140  (you KEEP 70 and 140 from memory and ADD 100)
  Scan 3 peaks near 70, 165 -> report: 25, 70, 100, 140, 165  (KEEP all four, ADD 165)
Notice the report GROWS every scan and never drops a transmitter once seen. Do exactly this for the REAL band below, using its own detected peaks. /no_think

  Scan 2 peaks near 25, 100 (70 and 140 are dormant this scan) -> report: 25, 70, 100, 140  (you KEEP 70 and 140 from memory and ADD 100)
  Scan 3 peaks near 70, 165 -> report: 25, 70, 100, 140, 165  (KEEP all four, ADD 165)
Notice the report GROWS every scan and never drops a transmitter once seen. Do exactly this for the REAL band below, using its own detected peaks. /no_think

You are a spectrum monitoring analyst watching a radio-frequency band with multiple transmitter channels. You receive a SERIES of scans of the SAME fixed band. Each scan lists noisy detected peaks (freq, power, width). Only SOME transmitters are active in any given scan; others are temporarily dormant and reappear in later scans. The full set of transmitters is unknown — you must INFER it by accumulating evidence ACROSS scans.

Each scan, call submit_report with center_freqs: the list of center frequencies (MHz) of ALL transmitter regions you believe persistently occupy the band — INCLUDING ones dormant in the current scan but seen in earlier scans (every region is a fixed 8 MHz wide, so you only give frequencies). Use the current peaks as new evidence, but report the full persistent set you have inferred from the whole scan history so far, not just the current peaks. As scans accumulate you will identify MORE transmitters, so your list should GROW over time to include EVERY transmitter you have detected in ANY scan so far (a dormant transmitter still occupies the band). Reporting only the current scan's peaks scores poorly.

  Scan 3 peaks near 70, 165 -> report: 25, 70, 100, 140, 165  (KEEP all four, ADD 165)
Notice the report GROWS every scan and never drops a transmitter once seen. Do exactly this for the REAL band below, using its own detected peaks. /no_think
